evidence_quotes.quote: Regarding personalized advertising, the problem is that it is essentially spying on people in order to control their behavior.
A scalper can easily double the price or more.

paragraphs.text: This Act can do a lot of good for the EU and its citizens, but these issues existed for a long time and it is important to know why they persisted, so that they can be solved without needing an overly long list of complicated regulations. If we live in a free market economy, we should expect companies, which offer consumer unfriendly services full of deceptive and addictive design, to be soon replaced by new companies offering better service. But that does not happen nearly as much as it should. I can think of few reasons for that. First, some unethical designs give the company a competitive edge, namely the addictiveness and pressure on customers to pay. Second, companies try to suppress competition by locking customers to their product. For example, companies like Microsoft or Apple can limit what software can be installed on their operating systems and thus force customers to use their own paid software solutions. Unfortunately, governments can also contribute to this by for example creating mandatory mobile apps, which support only Android and iPhone, thus making it very hard for the market to create a new better alternative. Companies can also make their previous product unusable using software updates and thus force customers to pay for a new one. This can happen for physical electronic devices also, not just pure software, as long as there is a microchip with access to internet it can be broken remotely, resulting in unnecessary electronic waste. Finally, some businesses can be really hard to start due to extra regulation, that was not really necessary in the first place, but now it helps to protect existing companies from competition. So, while many of the deceptive and addictive designs require regulation, the problem as a whole is also caused by lack of competition due to overlooked anticompetitive practices. Some of the deceptive designs mentioned in the Act are already illegal, but the law is not effectively enforced. Issue is not in how to find out about a company breaking the law, customers will complain if there is a government portal for that. Issue is the actual enforcement. Sometimes this is because the punishment is not severe enough. That usually manifests as a company getting a penalty, which pales in comparison with their profits, so it becomes a cost of doing business for them. Of special note are dating services, which use a lot of deceptive and addictive designs, but they would not be economically viable without them. If they work well, they will loose customers too quickly after gaining them. Instead they benefit from people breaking up and spending a long time in the dating app. By their nature they also know more about a countrys citizens than their own government. An effective, public, government financed dating service might be beneficial both in protecting personal information of the citizens and increasing reproduction by helping people find stable relationships. Regarding personalized advertising, the problem is that it is essentially spying on people in order to control their behavior. Just instead of putting people in prison for what they say online, it pushes them to buy a particular product. Given how detailed the data can be, there is a big potential for misuse, if it gets in the wrong hands. It can also be argued that seeing intrusive product advertisement everywhere all the time, incentivizes customers to buy more than they need, leading to wasting resources, which goes against EUs environmental goals. Scalping should be straight up illegal, because it allows scalpers to earn money without really contributing to the economy. However, it is truly hard to define. The only real difference between a scalper and a shop is the margin. A shop buys from a factory, moves the product on their shelves and puts maybe 20% higher price on it. A scalper can easily double the price or more. Limiting the price increase on resale of freshly made products is the most robust solution I can think of
